topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper lay spread across the kitchen table, the fluorescent light above casting a harsh glare on the unfamiliar genetic markers. Elias traced the lines with a trembling finger, his breath catching in shallow gasps. Every word in the report felt like a physical blow, shattering the carefully constructed reality he’d inhabited for two decades. He stared at the DNA match percentages, a cold dread pooling in his stomach. He pushed the chair back, the scrape of wood against tile sounding deafening in the silence of the apartment. He didn't look at Sarah, who was humming softly in the living room, and the simple act of moving toward her felt monumental, an unbearable crossing of an unseen chasm.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." The words were a choked whisper, barely audible above the drumming pulse in her ears. Maya reread the final section of the report for the fifth time, her eyes blurring slightly. The ink seemed to swim before her. A strange, hollow sensation settled deep in her chest, heavy and suffocating. She crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a small, sharp tearing noise. Her hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against the cheap paper. She retreated to the bathroom, leaning against the cool porcelain sink, trying to steady the rapid flutter in her chest, the world outside the tiled room suddenly too loud, too sharp.

[story 3]

He sat on the edge of the bed, the unfamiliar texture of the duvet rough beneath his fingertips. The phone lay face down on the nightstand, a silent accusation. When he finally picked it up, his thumb hovered over the contact labeled 'Family.' He didn't dial. Instead, he scrolled through old photos, each smiling face suddenly seeming alien, disconnected from the man staring back at him in the mirror. A tremor ran through his hands as he dropped the phone onto the carpet. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the room, his movements jerky and uncoordinated, unable to settle his gaze on any single point. The air in the room felt thick, resistant to his presence.
